summary: High‑energy Red Rocks set with soaring leads & tight jams
blurb: The show delivers a fiery first set highlighted by Hell In A Bucket and Bird Song, then builds to a climactic Terrapin Station → Drums → Space sequence in the second set. Jerry’s soaring guitar and Brent’s inspired keyboards drive the performance, making it a must‑listen.
review: Red Rocks on August 12, 1987 captured one of the Dead’s most electrifying late‑‘80s outings. The first set kicked off with a punchy Hell In A Bucket that segued into an impassioned Sugaree and a razor‑sharp Bird Song, setting a high‑energy tone that never let up. Highlights like Never Trust A Woman showcased Brent’s inventive keyboard voicings while his vocal flair lifted Cumberland later in the night. The second set was pure fireworks – a seamless China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider transition gave way to an expansive Terrapin Station that exploded into a thunderous Drums → Space, culminating in an otherworldly stretch of The Other One before winding down with a heartfelt Wharf Rat and a sing‑along Mighty Quinn. Across the board Jerry’s guitar was “on fire,” delivering soaring leads and the iconic “Cool Colorado Rain” line, while Mickey’s drumming added complex intensity that pierced through the mix. With strong bass thunder from Phil and solid rhythmic foundation from Billy, this performance stands out as a high‑octane snapshot of the band in top form.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All six recordings report positive sentiment, repeatedly praising the band’s energy, Jerry’s soaring leads, Brent’s keyboard work, and the climactic Terrapin Station/Drums/Space sequence. Multiple sources highlight the same standout songs (Bird Song, The Other One, Wharf Rat) and describe the performance as “fiery,” “high‑energy,” and “on a roll,” indicating strong, consistent acclaim across recordings.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard capture with crisp, clear transfer; highlights all key moments and corrects minor pitch issue, providing the most faithful listening experience.

key_highlights: Fiery first set that never loses momentum, anchored by Hell In A Bucket and Bird Song.
Terrapin Station → Drums → Space sequence builds to a climactic peak in the second set.
Brent Mydland’s inspired keyboards and vocals (Never Trust A Woman, Cumberland).
Mickey Hart’s intense drumming during Drums > Space adds extra propulsion.

band_performance.Jerry: Soaring leads dominate the night – from the iconic “Cool Colorado Rain” line in Rider to high‑pitched, on‑the‑roll solos throughout, especially during The Other One and Terrapin Station.
band_performance.Phil: Beautiful bass tone with audible thunder; strong interplay with Brent’s keyboards and solid foundation for the band’s high‑energy flow.
band_performance.Bob: Vocal power shines on Sun‑type numbers and Bird Song; despite a slightly weaker voice noted on some tracks, his rhythm work remains solid.
band_performance.Brent: Inspired first‑set performance (Yankee Doodle tuning, Never Trust A Woman) and tinkling keyboards that lift Cumberland; strong vocal moments add extra color.
band_performance.Mickey: Complex, intense drumming that shines during the Drums → Space segment, described as “piercing your lungs” and driving the second‑set momentum.
